articles.headline: Mental and Moral Improvement Association of Troy -- new honorary members and lyceum topics
articles.summary: Peter Jackson, Corresponding Secretary of the Mental and Moral Improvement Association of Troy, NY, publishes the Association's newly elected honorary members: Rev. Samuel E. Cornish, Rev. Christopher Rush, Rev. Charles W. Gardner, Rev. William Miller, Rev. William Douglass, Rev. Peter Williams, Rt. Rev. Morris Brown, and Rev. Edward Waters. Notes the Association's officers (Rev. Daniel A. Payne President, Rev. Daniel Underwood and William Rich Vice Presidents, Peter Jackson Corresponding Secretary, George H. Baltimore Recording Secretary, Alexander Truly Treasurer -- the same slate that hosted the Oct. 3 Dr. Smith reception) and lists lyceum essay topics delivered by members (George H. Baltimore on temperance, Clarence B. Selden on liberty, Moses B. Vantere on education), plus the debate question for the next meeting: whether law or prejudice is more injurious to the colored people.

articles.headline: Fugitive-slave arrest of a Maryland-born man before Justice Bloodgood, referred to Judge Betts
articles.summary: Mr. M'Pherson of Frederick County, MD, with a slave-catcher's aid, seized a man in New York whom he claimed as property, born in his family and absconded in October 1836. Brought before Justice Bloodgood, who declined jurisdiction and referred the matter to Judge Betts of the U.S. Circuit Court; abolitionist counsel Mr. Dresser intervened once informed. The case was pending at time of writing, alongside the still-unresolved Dixon case.

articles.headline: Sketch of Churches and Ecclesiastical Organizations among the People of Color -- Union (Methodist) Church of Wilmington, Delaware
articles.summary: Continuing series on Black church bodies: recounts the 1805 founding of the Union Church of Africa in Wilmington, DE, led by Rev. Peter Spencer, after white trustees denied the Black congregation a voice in church governance -- paralleling the same-era Philadelphia/New York/African Methodist Episcopal secessions. Prints full membership/hearer statistics tables by circuit for the AME (Rev. Christopher Rush, Superintendant; Rev. Wm. Miller, Senior Elder) and Union Church connexions (Rev. Peter Spencer, presiding Elder) across Delaware, Pennsylvania, New Jersey and New York -- 21 churches, ~1,263 total members recapitulated by state.

articles.headline: Mr. Gloucester's Fair -- New York congregation raises $700+ for a Philadelphia sister church's building debt
articles.summary: Recounts how a small New York congregation that seceded from Philadelphia's First Colored Presbyterian Church, worshipping on St. Mary Street, undertook a $3,000+ building expansion; after raising $1,800 toward their builder through subscriptions and a Philadelphia fair (netting $600), Mr. Gloucester organized a five-night fair in New York that raised over $700 (nearly all from the city's colored citizens) toward the remaining debt.

articles.headline: Mary Adams Bright's letter from Bangor, Maine -- subscriptions and a new ladies' abolition society
articles.author: Mary Adams Bright
articles.summary: Bright writes from Bangor, ME (Sept. 30, 1837) urging Cornish not to discontinue the paper, encloses $3 for two subscriptions (to Mrs. Charles Plummer and to herself), and reports that after finding Bangor's abolition 'corps' largely disbanded, the city's women -- crediting them with reviving the cause though they seek no credit -- are organizing a new anti-slavery society and hope to subscribe for copies of the paper.

articles.headline: Wm. A. Gibbs's caution to colored travellers -- the execution of George Davis in Havana
articles.author: Wm. A. Gibbs
articles.summary: Writing from Matanzas, Cuba (Sept. 1837), Gibbs reports that George Davis, a free Black American tailor, was arrested in Havana on suspicion of abolitionism after abolition books and papers were found in his trunk, given no trial, and executed (garroted -- 'screwed to death') on Sept. 1837 order of Governor Tacon. A white man suspected of being an abolition book agent was also jailed awaiting trial. Gibbs warns colored Americans against travelling to Cuba, since any found there are imprisoned, sold into perpetual slavery if convicted, or held until a slave ship is ready to remove them if not.

articles.headline: Governor Tacon denounced for the execution of George Davis
articles.summary: Follow-up editorial note ('Savage Barbarity') expressing sympathy for the family of the murdered George Davis and condemning Cuban Governor Tacon's 'barbarous' conduct in condemning an innocent man to death without trial.

articles.headline: Congress: petitions against Texas annexation
articles.summary: Reports that John Quincy Adams presented a petition from upwards of 3,000 women of Boston against the annexation of Texas to the United States, to be printed alongside an earlier petition from women of Halifax, VA (presented by Mr. Wise) praying Congress to protect southern rights against northern 'fanaticism.' Mr. Cushing separately presented over 100 memorials against Texas annexation.

articles.headline: Full proceedings: New York colored citizens' reception of Dr. James McCune Smith
articles.summary: Full text of the Sept. 26, 1837 Tabernacle Lecture Room reception welcoming Dr. James McCune Smith home from five years' medical study at the University of Glasgow (companion piece to the brief notice in CA_1837-09-23). Confirms Ransom F. Wake as President, with John Brown and John Peterson as Vice Presidents; prints Wake's welcoming address recounting Smith's exclusion from an American medical school by prejudice and his subsequent Glasgow education among the Wardlaws and Buchanans, resolutions of welcome and confidence in his professional ability, and Smith's own lengthy reply reflecting on his exile, his grief at the death of William Hamilton, and his gratitude to the meeting; also records a second address by Thomas S. Sidney.

articles.headline: Prejudice in the Church, continued -- grievances of colored Presbyterian worshippers
articles.summary: Continuing series cataloguing discrimination against Black congregants in white churches: refusal to rent pews to colored members compelling long Sabbath walks to a colored church, and a personal account of being seated in an obscure side aisle at Rev. Dr. Cox's church (Laight Street, then Rev. Mr. Mines's) by the politeness of Mr. Tappan, provoking a 'perfect tumult' among the white congregation.

articles.headline: Thomas Van Rensselaer revealed as a formerly enslaved man, warmly received by his former Southern master
articles.summary: Reprinted from the Emancipator: recounts an unnamed runaway slave's visit to his former Southern master, who -- learning his former bondman had become a respectable, prosperous citizen -- received him as a guest at his table and in his carriage, showed him to neighbors, and gave him the original runaway hand-bill as a keepsake, saying he was glad he had not recaptured him. The piece reveals the subject to be 'our worthy and much esteemed friend, Mr. THOMAS VAN RENSSLAER,' corner of William and Ann Streets, New York -- confirming Van Rensselaer's own history of enslavement and escape.

articles.headline: Augustus Wattles's letter on colored settlers taking up farmland in Indiana
articles.author: Augustus Wattles (forwarding a letter from John Wattles)
articles.summary: Wattles forwards a letter from his brother John Wattles, writing from Cabin Creek, Indiana (Sept. 21, 1837), describing colored settlers becoming independent landowners -- including Thos. Robinson, a shoemaker who emigrated from Virginia a year earlier, now renting a farm, working his trade, owning a town lot, and teaching his own children -- as evidence that colored men can achieve the same independence as white settlers through farming, continuing the Ohio survey work noted in earlier Wattles correspondence.

articles.headline: Colored Orphan Asylum -- detailed visit report
articles.summary: Detailed follow-up report (after the founding notices in CA_1837-04-22/04-29) on a personal visit to the Asylum for Colored Orphans, 12th Street between 5th and 6th Avenues, a building bequeathed by the late Dr. McLeod and capable of housing 60+ children; praises the matron and an elderly colored nurse, and urges the Directresses to designate a regular visiting day for colored citizens to bring monetary, clothing, and provision donations in an orderly way.

articles.headline: Rev. James W. C. Pennington reports a new house of worship, 'Mount of Zion,' seating 180
articles.author: James W. C. Pennington
articles.summary: Pennington reports the dedication of a new house for divine worship, seating about 180, with his co-presbyter Rev. John Goldsmith; no church has yet been formally organized there, with members of several churches to worship jointly, and Pennington engaged to preach.

articles.headline: Libel suit -- John Russell sues Cornish and Ruggles over the Gaving letter on African kidnapping
articles.summary: Reports that the editor and publisher have been sued for libel by Mr. John Russell over the earlier-published letter from Mr. Gaving to D. Ruggles alleging Russell fraudulently shipped three native Africans south for sale; the paper states it printed the letter on respectable authority and public duty, and that its columns remained open to Russell to contradict it, calling the suit an attempt to harass rather than correct the record.

articles.headline: Marriages and a death, October 1837
articles.summary: Marriages: Henry Wilson to Mary Carter (Lancaster, PA, Oct. 1, by Rev. Henry C. Turner); George Corr of Chester Co. to Mrs. Susannah Neilson of Columbia, PA (Oct. 3, same officiant); Stephen Jackson of Clarkstown, NY to Martha Ann Wells, eldest daughter of Rev. J. Wells (Newark, Oct. 22, by Rev. James Simmons). Death: Henry Nichols, in his 44th year, Oct. 24.

articles.headline: Charles B. Ray's travel letter -- the colored community of Buffalo and the Female Dorcas Society
articles.author: Charles B. Ray
articles.summary: General Agent Ray reports from Lockport, NY (Oct. 21, 1837) on nine days in Buffalo: obtained 32 paid subscribers and roughly $100 for the paper from Buffalo's colored citizens (population 500-700), praises the community's 'profound respectability,' and describes two religious societies, one benevolent society, and three literary societies (one female, two male -- juvenile and adult) engaged in winter debating. Names the Female Dorcas Society's officers who donated $10 toward the editor's salary and paper expenses: Mrs. Elizabeth Malvin (President), Mrs. Sydna E. R. A. M. Francis (Secretary), and Mrs. Payton Harris (Treasurer).

articles.headline: Philomathean Society anniversary -- Dr. John Brown inaugurated Prefect
articles.summary: Anniversary exercises of the Philomathean Society, whose membership includes two regularly educated physicians and two theological students. Dr. John Brown was inaugurated Prefect and delivered an inaugural address on the value of education; Dr. James McCune Smith addressed the audience for twenty minutes on the importance of thorough education, citing the deficiencies of the 'Oneida Institute' course and urging members to become scholars 'in reality' and not merely in name. The Society announced a winter/spring lecture course by its best-qualified members.

articles.headline: P. A. Bell relinquishes proprietorship of the Colored American to a Committee
articles.author: Philip A. Bell
articles.summary: Bell announces that circumstances have made it necessary for the permanent existence of the Colored American that he relinquish the Proprietorship to a Committee of colored gentlemen, who have assumed responsibility and will continue publication -- the first announcement of the reorganization completed and formally documented the following issue (CA_1837-11-11) under Thomas L. Jennings as Chairman and Charles B. Ray as Secretary.

articles.headline: Rev. S. H. Gloucester thanks New York supporters of the Philadelphia church fair
articles.author: S. H. Gloucester
articles.summary: Gloucester thanks New York and Brooklyn supporters of his Philadelphia congregation's fair (see CA_1837-10-21 'Mr. Gloucester's Fair'), which raised about $700, singling out editor Samuel E. Cornish for his recommendation of the fair to New York's benevolent public, Mr. William P. Johnson for hospitality, Mrs. Lane for her attention at the Fair, and Mr. Thomas Downing, George Downing, Thomas L. Jennings, Thomas Van Rensselaer, T. H. Sipkins, and Rev. Theodore S. Wright among others for their assistance; also thanks Mr. Wm. Brown of Brooklyn for medical assistance.

articles.headline: Julius C. Morel's letter to Frederick A. Hinton on retaining the term 'Colored'
articles.author: Julius C. Morel
articles.summary: Writing from Harrisburg (Sept. 10, 1837) to Frederick A. Hinton, Morel argues in favor of retaining the term 'Colored Man' in the organization's proceedings, rejecting the notion that the term implies mental or moral inferiority, and citing the Society of Friends' embrace of the originally derisive 'Quaker' label as a precedent for owning rather than discarding a group name.

articles.headline: Formal notice: the Colored American placed under a Committee of colored gentlemen
articles.author: Thomas L. Jennings; Charles B. Ray
articles.summary: Full formal notice (following P. A. Bell's Nov. 4 announcement) that the Colored American -- with 1,200-1,500 subscribers -- has been placed on a permanent basis under a Committee of colored gentlemen who pledge themselves for its future publication, signed by Thomas L. Jennings as Chairman and Charles B. Ray as Secretary; announces that Dr. James McCune Smith will contribute a series of letters on his five years in Europe (excerpts begin this issue as 'Dr. Smith's Journal'), and that Isaiah DeGrasse will attend to the editorial department during Ray's upcoming trip to Philadelphia.

articles.headline: Marriages and a benefit fair, November 1837
articles.summary: Marriages: Thomas Smith of Philadelphia to Elizabeth Conner (New York, Oct. 22, by Rev. Peter Williams); Theodore C. Breshow Vedal to Henrietta W. Bolt (New York, Oct. 26, by Rev. Peter Williams). Separately, a four-day fair opened Nov. 13 at the 7th Ward Hotel for the benefit of the African Methodist Episcopal Bethel Church, Second Street, managed by Martha Low (Principal) and Ann M. Johnson, under Rev. William Cornish, Elder in charge.

events.name: Mr. Gloucester's five-night fundraising fair for the Philadelphia offshoot congregation's church debt -- New York
events.description: A five-evening fair organized by Mr. Gloucester in New York City on behalf of a small congregation (seceded from Philadelphia's First Colored Presbyterian Church, worshipping on St. Mary Street, Philadelphia) to help pay off a builder's debt for enlarging their church; raised over $700, almost entirely from New York's colored citizens.

events.name: Philomathean Society anniversary exercises -- inauguration of Dr. John Brown as Prefect
events.description: Anniversary exercises of the Philomathean Society; Dr. John Brown inaugurated Prefect and delivered an inaugural address; Dr. James McCune Smith addressed the audience on education. The Society announced a winter lecture course by its most qualified members.

events.name: Reorganization of the Colored American under a publishing Committee
events.description: Formal reorganization transferring proprietorship of the Colored American from Philip A. Bell to a Committee of colored gentlemen who pledged themselves for its continued publication, with 1,200-1,500 subscribers reported at the time.

events.name: Female Dorcas Society of Buffalo -- donation to the Colored American
events.description: The Female Dorcas Society of Buffalo donated $10 toward the Colored American's editorial salary and incidental expenses, reported in Charles B. Ray's travel letter describing Buffalo's colored community of two religious societies, one benevolent society, and three literary societies.
attendees: Sydna E. R. A. M. Francis
